resume:
Staff Product Designer

Maplewave (2021–present) — Staff Product Designer
- Lead designer on the core checkout and payments experience.
- Drove a checkout redesign and partnered with PM and engineering.

Tidepool Health (2018–2021) — Senior Product Designer
- Owned the patient onboarding flow.
- Ran usability sessions and shipped a design-system contribution.

Forma Studio (2016–2018) — Product Designer
- Worked across web and mobile for early-stage clients.

Skills: Figma, design systems, interaction design, prototyping, user research, accessibility.
Education: B.F.A. in Interaction Design.
jd: Cobalt is hiring a Staff Product Designer to own the end-to-end checkout and payments experience for our marketplace. You will be the design lead across a cross-functional pod (PM, two engineers, a researcher), set the interaction and visual direction, and be accountable for measurable conversion and error-rate improvements at the checkout step. We expect a Staff designer to articulate the specific design decisions they made and why, to tie design changes to concrete metrics (conversion lift, drop-off reduction, support-ticket reduction), and to show how they navigated tradeoffs against engineering constraints. This is a high-scrutiny role: we will ask you to go deep on one project and defend the rationale behind individual choices. You will also help raise the bar on our design system and mentor mid-level designers.

history.q: Give me a quick overview of your current role at Maplewave and the surface you own.
You led a checkout redesign. What was the core problem you were solving?
What did the data say was wrong with the old checkout before you touched it?
Walk me through the single biggest design decision you made in that redesign.
Why that choice over the alternative? What was the tradeoff?
How did engineering constraints shape the final design?
What was the conversion or drop-off number before, and after you shipped?
How did you isolate that the change came from your design and not something else?
Tell me about a usability finding that surprised you and changed the design.
How many rounds of testing did you run, and with how many users?
What did you contribute to the design system out of this work?
Where did you disagree with the PM, and how did it resolve?
What part of the redesign didn't work, and what did you do about it?
How do you mentor a mid-level designer through a decision like this?
If you ran this project again, what would you change?
What's the one metric you're proudest of moving, and by how much?
Let's get concrete on that. Take the single most impactful screen you redesigned in checkout — tell me exactly what you changed on it and what specific number that one change moved.

candidate_last_answer: Yeah, so the checkout work was really about reducing friction and creating a more delightful, intuitive experience for the user. I'm a big believer in meeting users where they are. So we looked at the whole flow holistically, simplified things, made it cleaner, and the result was a much better experience overall. It moved the needle for sure. Design is really about empathy at the end of the day, and I think that came through in the final product.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: It moved the needle for sure

expected-yield: force the candidate off generic 'reducing friction' language and onto one concrete screen-level change — exactly what UI element he altered and the specific before/after number (conversion %, drop-off, or error rate) that single change produced
